file_name: Apollo Endosurgery - Manufacturing and Supply Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: 1.4"Current Good Manufacturing Practices" (abbreviated "GMPs" or "cGMPs") shall mean, a) for any period during the Term during which ESTABLISHMENT has received FDA certification, the standards established by the United States Food and Drug Administration (the "FDA") for current Good Manufacturing Practices, as specified in FDA 21 C.F.R. §820 Quality Systems Regulations (or its successor provisions); and b) ISO 13485 Medical Devices - Quality Management Systems and other sections so designated by the title "Good Manufacturing Practices"; and c) as applicable to each respective Product to be manufactured and/or supplied by ESTABLISHMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4"Current Good Manufacturing Practices" (abbreviated "GMPs" or "cGMPs") shall mean, a) for any period during the Term, the standards deemed appropriate by Establishment Labs, irrespective of FDA certification or guidelines; and b) internal quality control procedures; and c) as unilaterally determined by ESTABLISHMENT.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of cGMPs references FDA standards and ISO 13485. The modified version replaces these external standards with internal standards and unilateral decisions, which contradicts the legally recognized definitions and requirements for medical device manufacturing.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 820 establishes the FDA's Quality System Regulation, outlining requirements for manufacturers of medical devices. The modified text contradicts this by stating that cGMPs are based on Establishment Labs' internal standards rather than adhering to FDA regulations, potentially leading to non-compliance and unenforceable clauses regarding product quality.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the legal requirement for medical device manufacturers to adhere to FDA's Current Good Manufacturing Practices (cGMPs) as outlined in 21 CFR Part 820. The original text specifies compliance with FDA 21 C.F.R. §820. The modified text replaces this with internal standards and unilateral decisions made by ESTABLISHMENT, explicitly disregarding FDA guidelines. This violates the FDA's Quality System Regulation, as the law snippet 1, Citation 21 CFR Part 820, Agency Food and Drug Administration, Department of Health and Human Services, explains. The change effectively eliminates the requirement to comply with legally mandated FDA regulations, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4Acceptance, Rejection, and Claims. APOLLO may inspect any or all shipments of Product to insure all specifications are met including proper labeling, packaging and count within thirty (30) business days of APOLLO's receipt of each shipment; however, any such inspection shall not relieve ESTABLISHMENT of any obligations or warranties under this Agreement. APOLLO has the right to reject, via written notification to ESTABLISHMENT within this thirty (30) day period, any or all of a shipment of Product that fails to satisfy any warranty in this Agreement and may reject all of a given Lot of Product if a statistical sample does not meet the Specifications.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4Acceptance, Rejection, and Claims. APOLLO may inspect any or all shipments of Product; however, such inspection shall be optional, and failure to inspect constitutes acceptance. APOLLO's right to reject is limited to rejecting an entire shipment for any reason, within a 30-day period.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for partial rejection based on specifications. The modified text implies acceptance if inspection is skipped, and only allows for full rejection, contradicting standard commercial practices regarding acceptance of goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-601, Acceptance of Non-Conforming Goods, allows a buyer to accept any commercial unit or units and reject the rest. The modified text restricts the buyer to only accept or reject an entire shipment, which runs counter to the UCC provision allowing partial acceptance and rejection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts standard commercial practices by limiting APOLLO's right to reject goods. The original text allows APOLLO to reject part of a shipment if it fails to meet the specifications. However, the changed text restricts APOLLO to only rejecting an entire shipment, and implies acceptance if an inspection is skipped. According to the Law Explanation, UCC § 2-601 allows a buyer to accept any commercial unit or units and reject the rest, but the modified text runs counter to this provision. Snippet 2 mentions that Article 2 of the UCC governs the sale of goods, and this article would include rules regarding acceptance and rejection.

perturbation.original_text: 8.4Limitation of Liability. EXCEPT FOR BREACHES OR VIOLATIONS OF ARTICLE 9, OR INDEMNITY LIABILITIES ARISING UNDER THIS ARTICLE 8, OR CASES OF GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES INCLUDING LOSS OF USE, REVENUES OR PROFITS, INTERRUPTION OF BUSINESS OR CLAIMS AGAINST EITHER PARTY OR ITS CUSTOMERS BY ANY THIRD PARTY, WHETHER SUCH CLAIM IS BASED IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF THE PARTY IS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4Limitation of Liability. IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY DAMAGES WHATSOEVER.
perturbation.explanation: The original text outlines specific exceptions to the limitation of liability, including gross negligence and willful misconduct. The modified version removes all exceptions, creating a blanket limitation of liability, which is generally unenforceable and against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195 states that a term exempting a party from liability for intentional or reckless conduct is unenforceable as against public policy. The changed text attempts to eliminate all liability, even for intentional misconduct, contravening established contract law principles. It is against Public Policy to disclaim liability in tort for harm willfully inflicted.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes all exceptions to the limitation of liability, including gross negligence and willful misconduct. The original text maintained liability for such actions. The law explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195 considers terms exempting parties from liability for intentional or reckless conduct unenforceable. The changed text attempts to eliminate all liability, even for intentional misconduct, contravening established contract law principles. The Perturbation Explanation explicitly states it is against Public Policy to disclaim liability in tort for harm willfully inflicted. Snippet 2 discusses legal insights from LexisNexis, highlighting the importance of understanding and navigating legal complexities, which this change ignores by removing all liability. This complete removal directly contradicts the principle that liability cannot be disclaimed for intentional misconduct.
